name: Physical Education
description: Foundational concepts in physical fitness, exercise science, and lifelong wellness habits. Students explore the five components of fitness, movement skills, sport participation, and healthy lifestyle design.

modules.title: Introduction to Physical Fitness
modules.objectives: Define physical fitness; identify its five health-related components; understand why each matters for lifelong health. Students will connect concepts to safe practice, explain risks or benefits, and document evidence of understanding in the module assignment.
modules.readingNote: CDC — Physical Activity Basics — for background reading on Introduction to Physical Fitness before the quiz and assignment
modules.videoNote: GIIS Learn Portal — use the module lesson, assigned course materials, and teacher-reviewed practice inside the GIIS course path; no external practice platform is required.
modules.practiceNote: GIIS Learn Portal — use the module lesson, assigned course materials, and teacher-reviewed practice inside the GIIS course path; no external practice platform is required.
modules.assignment: Assess your current fitness level using all 5 components. Create a personal fitness goal statement and a one-week exercise plan. Submit your work as a labeled artifact that includes: (1) at least three specific examples from the lesson, (2) a short explanation of your reasoning for each major choice, (3) one correction or revision based on self-check, and (4) a 100-word reflection connecting the skill to a real academic, career, or life situation. Submit: a document, worksheet, slide, image, or link that shows the completed work. Include: notes, examples, evidence, calculations, sources, or analysis from the Introduction to Physical Fitness module, plus a short explanation of how your work supports your answer. Evaluation: the teacher should be able to see the final product, the steps or evidence used, and one clear connection to the module objective.

modules.title: The FITT Principle
modules.objectives: Explain Frequency, Intensity, Time, and Type; apply the FITT principle to design a safe and effective personal workout program. Students will connect concepts to safe practice, explain risks or benefits, and document evidence of understanding in the module assignment.
modules.readingNote: GIIS Learn Portal — use the module lesson, assigned course materials, and teacher-reviewed practice inside the GIIS course path; no external practice platform is required.
modules.videoNote: GIIS Learn Portal — use the module lesson, assigned course materials, and teacher-reviewed practice inside the GIIS course path; no external practice platform is required.
modules.practiceNote: GIIS Learn Portal — use the module lesson, assigned course materials, and teacher-reviewed practice inside the GIIS course path; no external practice platform is required.
modules.assignment: Design a 4-week workout program using the FITT principle targeting at least two fitness components. Justify each variable choice. Submit your work as a labeled artifact that includes: (1) at least three specific examples from the lesson, (2) a short explanation of your reasoning for each major choice, (3) one correction or revision based on self-check, and (4) a 100-word reflection connecting the skill to a real academic, career, or life situation. Submit: a document, worksheet, slide, image, or link that shows the completed work. Include: notes, examples, evidence, calculations, sources, or analysis from the The FITT Principle module, plus a short explanation of how your work supports your answer. Evaluation: the teacher should be able to see the final product, the steps or evidence used, and one clear connection to the module objective.

modules.title: Cardiovascular Endurance
modules.objectives: Define cardiovascular endurance; explain heart and lung responses to aerobic exercise; identify activities that build aerobic fitness. Students will connect concepts to safe practice, explain risks or benefits, and document evidence of understanding in the module assignment.
modules.readingNote: CDC — Physical Activity for Adults — for background reading on Cardiovascular Endurance before the quiz and assignment
modules.videoNote: GIIS Learn Portal — use the module lesson, assigned course materials, and teacher-reviewed practice inside the GIIS course path; no external practice platform is required.
modules.practiceNote: GIIS Learn Portal — use the module lesson, assigned course materials, and teacher-reviewed practice inside the GIIS course path; no external practice platform is required.
modules.assignment: Track your resting heart rate for 5 days. Complete 3 aerobic sessions; record distance, time, and perceived exertion after each. Submit your work as a labeled artifact that includes: (1) at least three specific examples from the lesson, (2) a short explanation of your reasoning for each major choice, (3) one correction or revision based on self-check, and (4) a 100-word reflection connecting the skill to a real academic, career, or life situation. Submit: a document, worksheet, slide, image, or link that shows the completed work. Include: notes, examples, evidence, calculations, sources, or analysis from the Cardiovascular Endurance module, plus a short explanation of how your work supports your answer. Evaluation: the teacher should be able to see the final product, the steps or evidence used, and one clear connection to the module objective.

modules.title: Muscular Strength and Endurance
modules.objectives: Distinguish muscular strength from muscular endurance; identify major muscle groups; describe resistance training principles for each quality. Students will connect concepts to safe practice, explain risks or benefits, and document evidence of understanding in the module assignment.
modules.readingNote: NSCA — Strength Training 101 — for background reading on Muscular Strength and Endurance before the quiz and assignment
modules.videoNote: Muscular Strength vs. Endurance — for a visual explanation of Muscular Strength and Endurance before guided practice
modules.practiceNote: GIIS Learn Portal — use the module lesson, assigned course materials, and teacher-reviewed practice inside the GIIS course path; no external practice platform is required.
modules.assignment: Perform a push-up, sit-up, and wall-sit test. Record results and compare to age norms. Write a 4-week strength plan for one major muscle group. Submit: a document, worksheet, slide, image, or link that shows the completed work. Include: notes, examples, evidence, calculations, sources, or analysis from the Muscular Strength and Endurance module, plus a short explanation of how your work supports your answer. Evaluation: the teacher should be able to see the final product, the steps or evidence used, and one clear connection to the module objective.

modules.title: Flexibility and Stretching
modules.objectives: Define flexibility; distinguish static, dynamic, and PNF stretching; explain how regular stretching reduces injury risk. Students will connect concepts to safe practice, explain risks or benefits, and document evidence of understanding in the module assignment.
modules.readingNote: GIIS Learn Portal — use the module lesson, assigned course materials, and teacher-reviewed practice inside the GIIS course path; no external practice platform is required.
modules.videoNote: Dynamic Warm-Up Routine — for a visual explanation of Flexibility and Stretching before guided practice
modules.practiceNote: GIIS Learn Portal — use the module lesson, assigned course materials, and teacher-reviewed practice inside the GIIS course path; no external practice platform is required.
modules.assignment: Perform a sit-and-reach flexibility test. Develop a 10-minute daily stretching routine. Record range-of-motion improvements over one week. Submit your work as a labeled artifact that includes: (1) at least three specific examples from the lesson, (2) a short explanation of your reasoning for each major choice, (3) one correction or revision based on self-check, and (4) a 100-word reflection connecting the skill to a real academic, career, or life situation. Submit: a document, worksheet, slide, image, or link that shows the completed work. Include: notes, examples, evidence, calculations, sources, or analysis from the Flexibility and Stretching module, plus a short explanation of how your work supports your answer. Evaluation: the teacher should be able to see the final product, the steps or evidence used, and one clear connection to the module objective.

modules.title: Body Composition
modules.objectives: Define body composition; explain lean mass vs. fat mass; describe healthy body fat ranges and assessment methods including BMI limitations.
modules.readingNote: CDC — Assessing Your Weight: BMI — for background reading on Body Composition before the quiz and assignment
modules.videoNote: GIIS Learn Portal — use the module lesson, assigned course materials, and teacher-reviewed practice inside the GIIS course path; no external practice platform is required.
modules.practiceNote: GIIS Learn Portal — use the module lesson, assigned course materials, and teacher-reviewed practice inside the GIIS course path; no external practice platform is required.
modules.assignment: Calculate your BMI and research your healthy body fat range. Discuss how exercise and diet affect body composition in a one-page reflection. Submit: a document, worksheet, slide, image, or link that shows the completed work. Include: notes, examples, evidence, calculations, sources, or analysis from the Body Composition module, plus a short explanation of how your work supports your answer. Evaluation: the teacher should be able to see the final product, the steps or evidence used, and one clear connection to the module objective.

modules.title: Goal Setting and Fitness Plans
modules.objectives: Apply the SMART goal framework to fitness goals; build a structured personal training plan with measurable outcomes and timelines. Students will connect concepts to safe practice, explain risks or benefits, and document evidence of understanding in the module assignment.
modules.readingNote: CDC — Adding Physical Activity to Your Life
modules.videoNote: SMART Goals for Fitness — for a visual explanation of Goal Setting and Fitness Plans before guided practice
modules.practiceNote: GIIS Learn Portal — use the module lesson, assigned course materials, and teacher-reviewed practice inside the GIIS course path; no external practice platform is required.
modules.assignment: Write 3 SMART fitness goals (short-, medium-, and long-term). Build an 8-week training calendar addressing each goal with specific workouts. Submit: a document, worksheet, slide, image, or link that shows the completed work. Include: notes, examples, evidence, calculations, sources, or analysis from the Goal Setting and Fitness Plans module, plus a short explanation of how your work supports your answer. Evaluation: the teacher should be able to see the final product, the steps or evidence used, and one clear connection to the module objective.

modules.title: Nutrition for Active People
modules.objectives: Identify macronutrients and their roles in exercise; explain pre- and post-workout nutrition strategies; apply hydration guidelines. Students will connect concepts to safe practice, explain risks or benefits, and document evidence of understanding in the module assignment.
modules.readingNote: GIIS Learn Portal — use the module lesson, assigned course materials, and teacher-reviewed practice inside the GIIS course path; no external practice platform is required.
modules.videoNote: GIIS Learn Portal — use the module lesson, assigned course materials, and teacher-reviewed practice inside the GIIS course path; no external practice platform is required.
modules.practiceNote: GIIS Learn Portal — use the module lesson, assigned course materials, and teacher-reviewed practice inside the GIIS course path; no external practice platform is required.
modules.assignment: Keep a 3-day food diary. Analyze macronutrient intake using a tracker. Write recommendations to improve your pre-workout nutrition. Submit your work as a labeled artifact that includes: (1) at least three specific examples from the lesson, (2) a short explanation of your reasoning for each major choice, (3) one correction or revision based on self-check, and (4) a 100-word reflection connecting the skill to a real academic, career, or life situation. Submit: a document, worksheet, slide, image, or link that shows the completed work. Include: notes, examples, evidence, calculations, sources, or analysis from the Nutrition for Active People module, plus a short explanation of how your work supports your answer. Evaluation: the teacher should be able to see the final product, the steps or evidence used, and one clear connection to the module objective.

quizQuestions.question: Which of the following is NOT one of the five health-related components of physical fitness?
quizQuestions.explanation: The five health-related components are cardiovascular endurance, muscular strength, muscular endurance, flexibility, and body composition. Agility is a skill-related component.

quizQuestions.question: The ability of the heart and lungs to deliver oxygen to working muscles over a prolonged period is called:
quizQuestions.explanation: Cardiovascular endurance reflects how efficiently the cardiorespiratory system sustains aerobic activity.

quizQuestions.question: Which option is not one of the five health-related components of physical fitness?
quizQuestions.explanation: Agility is a skill-related component, while the health-related components include cardiovascular endurance, muscular strength, muscular endurance, flexibility, and body composition.

quizQuestions.question: Short response: Which component involves the ability of a muscle to exert maximum force against resistance in a single effort?
quizQuestions.explanation: A correct response identifies "Muscular strength" and connects it to the module context.

quizQuestions.question: In the FITT principle, what does 'I' stand for?
quizQuestions.explanation: FITT = Frequency, Intensity, Time, Type — the four variables used to design an exercise program.

quizQuestions.question: 'Frequency' in the FITT principle refers to:
options: How often you exercise per week
What type of activity you do
quizQuestions.answer: How often you exercise per week
quizQuestions.explanation: Frequency refers to the number of exercise sessions per week. This reinforces the The FITT Principle idea behind the item instead of rewarding a surface-level guess.

quizQuestions.question: Which FITT recommendation is most appropriate for a beginner wanting to improve cardiovascular fitness?
options: 5 days/week at maximum intensity for 60 minutes
3 days/week at moderate intensity for 30 minutes
1 day/week at high intensity for 90 minutes
7 days/week at low intensity for 10 minutes
quizQuestions.answer: 3 days/week at moderate intensity for 30 minutes
quizQuestions.explanation: Beginners should start with moderate frequency and intensity to build fitness safely and reduce injury risk.

quizQuestions.question: In the FITT principle, what does the 'I' stand for?
quizQuestions.explanation: FITT stands for Frequency, Intensity, Time, and Type. Students need to recognize why Intensity matches the evidence in the question and why nearby options do not.

quizQuestions.question: In Cardiovascular Endurance, VO2 max is a measure of:
options: Maximum oxygen consumption during intense exercise
quizQuestions.answer: Maximum oxygen consumption during intense exercise
quizQuestions.explanation: VO2 max represents the maximum rate at which the body can consume oxygen during maximal aerobic exercise.

quizQuestions.question: A lower resting heart rate generally indicates:
quizQuestions.explanation: Well-trained hearts pump more blood per beat (greater stroke volume), beating fewer times per minute at rest.

quizQuestions.question: Which activity is primarily aerobic?
quizQuestions.explanation: Aerobic activities are sustained, moderate-intensity efforts using oxygen as the primary energy source.

quizQuestions.question: Which test most directly measures muscular endurance?
quizQuestions.explanation: Muscular endurance tests measure how many repetitions a muscle can perform over time, not maximum force.

quizQuestions.question: The principle of specificity in strength training means:
options: Train the same muscles every day
Training adaptations are specific to the muscles and movements trained
All exercises are equally effective for all muscles
Strength gains transfer automatically to all sports
quizQuestions.answer: Training adaptations are specific to the muscles and movements trained
quizQuestions.explanation: Specificity means the body adapts to the exact demands placed on it. The choice fits the prompt because it applies the central rule or concept from Muscular Strength and Endurance.

quizQuestions.question: Which muscle is primarily targeted by a push-up?
quizQuestions.explanation: Push-ups primarily work the pectoralis major (chest), assisted by the triceps and anterior deltoid.

quizQuestions.question: In Muscular Strength and Endurance, Hypertrophy refers to:
options: Increase in muscle fiber size due to training
quizQuestions.answer: Increase in muscle fiber size due to training
quizQuestions.explanation: Muscular hypertrophy is the enlargement of muscle fibers in response to resistance training.

quizQuestions.question: Static stretching is best performed:
options: Before explosive athletic activity as a warm-up
After exercise when muscles are warm
First thing in the morning before any activity
quizQuestions.answer: After exercise when muscles are warm
quizQuestions.explanation: Static stretching is most effective and safest post-exercise when muscles are warmed up and pliable.

quizQuestions.question: Dynamic stretching involves:
options: Controlled movements through a full range of motion
A partner pushing your limb past its range
quizQuestions.answer: Controlled movements through a full range of motion
quizQuestions.explanation: Dynamic stretching uses controlled movements like leg swings and arm circles to prepare muscles for activity.

quizQuestions.question: Static stretching is generally best performed when?
options: After exercise when muscles are warm
As the only warm-up before sprinting
quizQuestions.answer: After exercise when muscles are warm
quizQuestions.explanation: Static stretching is commonly used after activity or when muscles are warm.

quizQuestions.question: Dynamic stretching involves which kind of movement?
options: Controlled movement through a full range of motion
quizQuestions.answer: Controlled movement through a full range of motion
quizQuestions.explanation: Dynamic stretching uses movement; static stretching holds a position. The choice fits the prompt because it applies the central rule or concept from Flexibility and Stretching.

quizQuestions.question: In Body Composition, BMI is calculated as:
options: Fat mass divided by lean mass
quizQuestions.explanation: BMI = weight (kg) / height (m)^2. It is a simple screening proxy for body weight status.

quizQuestions.question: Which method provides the most accurate body fat percentage measurement?
quizQuestions.explanation: Hydrostatic weighing uses water displacement and Archimedes' principle to calculate body density and fat percentage accurately.

quizQuestions.question: Essential body fat is required for:
quizQuestions.explanation: Essential fat supports hormone production, organ protection, and other critical body functions.

quizQuestions.question: BMI is calculated using which relationship?
options: Body fat percentage divided by muscle mass
Resting heart rate divided by height
quizQuestions.explanation: BMI is a screening ratio of weight to height squared, not a direct body-fat measurement.

quizQuestions.question: The 'M' in SMART goals stands for:
quizQuestions.explanation: SMART = Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound. Measurable means clear criteria exist for tracking progress.

quizQuestions.question: Which is the best example of a SMART fitness goal?
options: I will run a 5K in under 30 minutes within 8 weeks by training 4 days per week
I want to be healthy someday
quizQuestions.answer: I will run a 5K in under 30 minutes within 8 weeks by training 4 days per week
quizQuestions.explanation: This goal is specific (5K), measurable (under 30 min), achievable, relevant (fitness), and time-bound (8 weeks).

quizQuestions.question: What does the 'M' in SMART goals stand for?
quizQuestions.explanation: SMART goals are Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, and Time-bound. The choice fits the prompt because it applies the central rule or concept from Goal Setting and Fitness Plans.

quizQuestions.question: Carbohydrates are the preferred energy source for high-intensity exercise because they:
options: Are quickly broken down into glucose for immediate energy
Provide more energy per gram than fat
quizQuestions.answer: Are quickly broken down into glucose for immediate energy
quizQuestions.explanation: Carbohydrates are rapidly converted to glucose and glycogen, providing the primary fuel for high-intensity exercise.

quizQuestions.question: How much water should athletes generally drink before exercise?
quizQuestions.explanation: Pre-hydration guidelines recommend 17–20 oz of water 2 hours before exercise for optimal hydration.

quizQuestions.question: Protein is most important for athletes in order to:
options: Support muscle repair and growth after exercise
quizQuestions.answer: Support muscle repair and growth after exercise
quizQuestions.explanation: Protein provides amino acids essential for repairing exercise-damaged muscle tissue and building new fibers.

quizQuestions.question: Why are carbohydrates often the preferred fuel for high-intensity exercise?
options: They are quickly broken down into glucose for usable energy
They cannot be stored in the body
They replace the need for hydration
They are unrelated to exercise intensity
quizQuestions.answer: They are quickly broken down into glucose for usable energy
quizQuestions.explanation: Carbohydrates can be used quickly during higher-intensity activity. Students need to recognize why They are quickly broken down into glucose for usable energy matches the evidence in the question and why nearby options do not.

questions.question: Which of the following is a health-related component of physical fitness?
questions.answer: Cardiovascular endurance
questions.explanation: Health-related components: cardiovascular endurance, muscular strength, muscular endurance, flexibility, body composition.

questions.question: In the FITT principle, 'Time' refers to:
options: The hour of day you exercise
questions.answer: The duration of each session
questions.explanation: Time in FITT refers to how long each individual workout session lasts. Students need to recognize why The duration of each session matches the evidence in the question and why nearby options do not.

questions.question: A student performs 3 sets of 15 push-ups. This primarily develops:
questions.answer: Muscular endurance
questions.explanation: High-repetition, lower-resistance exercises primarily develop muscular endurance. The choice fits the prompt because it applies the central rule or concept from Physical Education midterm concepts.

questions.question: A common classroom estimate for maximum heart rate subtracts age from what number?
questions.explanation: The simple estimate often taught is 220 minus age, though individual values vary.

questions.question: Dynamic stretching differs from static stretching because dynamic stretching:
options: Uses controlled movement through a range of motion
questions.answer: Uses controlled movement through a range of motion
questions.explanation: Dynamic stretching involves purposeful movement, unlike static stretching which holds a fixed position.

questions.question: In Physical Education midterm concepts, BMI measures:
questions.answer: Weight relative to height squared
questions.explanation: BMI = weight (kg) / height (m)^2 — a screening proxy for body weight status.

questions.question: The overload principle states that to improve fitness you must:
options: Stress the body beyond its current level to produce adaptation
Keep all workouts the same intensity
Train at maximum intensity from day one
questions.answer: Stress the body beyond its current level to produce adaptation
questions.explanation: Progressive overload — gradually increasing demand — is the foundation of fitness improvement.

questions.question: During high-intensity exercise, the body's preferred fuel source is usually what?
questions.answer: Carbohydrate
questions.explanation: Carbohydrate is generally easier to use quickly during high-intensity effort.

questions.question: R.I.C.E. is applied immediately after:
options: An acute soft-tissue injury such as a sprain
questions.answer: An acute soft-tissue injury such as a sprain
questions.explanation: R.I.C.E. (Rest, Ice, Compress, Elevate) is immediate first aid for acute soft-tissue injuries.

questions.question: Sportsmanship includes which behavior?
options: Celebrating aggressively in an opponent's face
questions.answer: Acknowledging good play by opponents
questions.explanation: Good sportsmanship includes respect for opponents, officials, and the game.

questions.question: The sit-and-reach assessment tests:
questions.answer: Flexibility
questions.explanation: The sit-and-reach test measures flexibility, primarily of the hamstrings and lower back.

questions.question: Why do SMART goals help athletes plan improvement?
options: They provide clear, measurable direction and timelines
They remove the need for practice
They make goals vague on purpose
questions.answer: They provide clear, measurable direction and timelines
questions.explanation: SMART goals make progress easier to plan, monitor, and adjust. The choice fits the prompt because it applies the central rule or concept from Physical Education midterm concepts.

questions.question: In Physical Education midterm concepts, VO2 max measures:
options: Maximum oxygen uptake during intense aerobic exercise
questions.answer: Maximum oxygen uptake during intense aerobic exercise
questions.explanation: VO2 max is the gold-standard measure of aerobic fitness. Students need to recognize why Maximum oxygen uptake during intense aerobic exercise matches the evidence in the question and why nearby options do not.

questions.question: Muscular hypertrophy refers to:
options: Increased size of muscle fibers due to training
questions.answer: Increased size of muscle fibers due to training
questions.explanation: Hypertrophy is the enlargement of existing muscle fibers in response to resistance training.

questions.question: Which activity best develops cardiovascular endurance?
questions.answer: 45-minute cycling session
questions.explanation: Sustained aerobic activities like 45-minute cycling best develop cardiovascular endurance.

questions.question: What does the principle of specificity mean in fitness training?
options: Training adaptations are specific to the activity and demands practiced
All exercises improve all components equally
questions.answer: Training adaptations are specific to the activity and demands practiced
questions.explanation: Specificity means the body adapts to the particular type of training it receives.

questions.question: In Physical Education final concepts, DOMS typically occurs:
questions.answer: 24–72 hours after exercise
questions.explanation: DOMS results from micro-damage and inflammation in muscle fibers, peaking 1–3 days after intense or unaccustomed exercise.

questions.question: Essential body fat is necessary for:
options: Normal physiological functions including hormone production and organ protection
questions.answer: Normal physiological functions including hormone production and organ protection
questions.explanation: Essential fat supports critical body functions and cannot be eliminated without serious health consequences.

questions.question: Regular aerobic exercise benefits mental health primarily by:
options: Increasing endorphins and regulating stress hormones
questions.answer: Increasing endorphins and regulating stress hormones
questions.explanation: Aerobic exercise triggers endorphin release and lowers chronic cortisol, reducing anxiety and depression.

questions.question: What is a lifetime physical activity?
options: An activity that can be enjoyed across adulthood and different life stages
An activity allowed only in childhood
A sport that must be done at maximum intensity
A workout that requires professional competition
questions.answer: An activity that can be enjoyed across adulthood and different life stages
questions.explanation: Lifetime activities are sustainable options people can continue as they age.

questions.question: Sleep is crucial for recovery because:
options: It eliminates the need to train
Growth hormone peaks during deep sleep, promoting muscle repair
Sleep burns more calories than exercise
questions.answer: Growth hormone peaks during deep sleep, promoting muscle repair
questions.explanation: Most growth hormone secretion and muscle repair occur during slow-wave (deep) sleep.

questions.question: Glycogen stores are primarily depleted during:
questions.answer: Prolonged moderate-to-high intensity exercise
questions.explanation: Glycogen is the primary fuel for moderate-to-high intensity exercise and becomes significantly depleted during prolonged sessions.

questions.question: The most important injury prevention strategy before explosive athletic activity is:
options: A dynamic warm-up including movement preparation
questions.answer: A dynamic warm-up including movement preparation
questions.explanation: Dynamic warm-ups increase heart rate, blood flow, and muscle temperature while activating neuromuscular patterns needed for sport.

questions.question: The FitnessGram PACER test primarily assesses which fitness component?
questions.answer: Aerobic capacity
questions.explanation: The PACER is a progressive aerobic endurance test. It connects the correct answer to Physical Education final concepts and separates it from plausible distractors.

questions.question: Which is the best SMART fitness goal?
options: Complete 10 more push-ups than my current max within 4 weeks, training 3 days/week
questions.answer: Complete 10 more push-ups than my current max within 4 weeks, training 3 days/week
questions.explanation: This goal is Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, and Time-bound. It connects the correct answer to Physical Education final concepts and separates it from plausible distractors.

questions.question: The reversibility principle states that:
options: Fitness gains decline when training stops
Training effects reverse after one missed session
Only beginners lose fitness when inactive
questions.answer: Fitness gains decline when training stops
questions.explanation: 'Use it or lose it' — fitness adaptations regress toward baseline when training is discontinued.

questions.question: Dehydration during exercise causes:
options: Decreased performance, heat illness risk, and impaired cognition
questions.answer: Decreased performance, heat illness risk, and impaired cognition
questions.explanation: Even mild dehydration (2% body weight loss) reduces aerobic performance and increases heat illness risk.

questions.question: A holistic wellness lifestyle attends to which dimensions?
options: Physical, mental, social, and emotional health dimensions
questions.answer: Physical, mental, social, and emotional health dimensions
questions.explanation: Holistic wellness considers multiple connected dimensions of health. It connects the correct answer to Physical Education final concepts and separates it from plausible distractors.

questions.question: PNF stretching achieves greater range of motion by:
options: Using alternating muscle contraction and relaxation
questions.answer: Using alternating muscle contraction and relaxation
questions.explanation: PNF exploits the neurological stretch reflex — alternating contraction and relaxation allows muscles to lengthen further.

questions.question: Team sports develop which skill beyond physical fitness?
questions.answer: Communication, collaboration, and leadership
questions.explanation: Team sports develop interpersonal skills alongside physical abilities. This reinforces the Physical Education final concepts idea behind the item instead of rewarding a surface-level guess.

questions.question: A cool-down after exercise is important because it:
options: Gradually reduces heart rate and helps remove metabolic waste
questions.answer: Gradually reduces heart rate and helps remove metabolic waste
questions.explanation: Cool-downs let the cardiovascular system transition safely to rest and help clear lactic acid from muscles.

questions.question: Which assessment best measures muscular strength?
questions.answer: One-repetition maximum (1RM) test
questions.explanation: A 1RM test estimates maximal force for a specific lift; the mile run measures aerobic endurance.

questions.question: Recommended sleep duration for teenagers is:
questions.answer: 8–10 hours per night
questions.explanation: Adolescents need 8–10 hours to support growth, recovery, cognitive function, and overall health.

questions.question: Which is an example of an aerobic activity?
questions.answer: 30-minute dance class
questions.explanation: Aerobic activities are sustained, moderate-intensity exercises relying on oxygen. A 30-minute dance class qualifies.

questions.question: Shin splints are best prevented by:
options: Gradually increasing load, wearing proper footwear, and cross-training
questions.answer: Gradually increasing load, wearing proper footwear, and cross-training
questions.explanation: Prevention includes the 10% rule (increase weekly mileage by no more than 10%), supportive shoes, and varied training surfaces.
